agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) with Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling problem that fundamentally changes the autonomy of both contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request implicitly proposes merging concerns that were deliberately separated.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer (>= 0) in Context A representing the current on-hand quantity of a Product in the warehouse."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer-initiated purchase request in Context B containing one or more OrderLines."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order referencing a Product by ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An item that exists in both contexts but with different meaning — in Context A it is a stockable inventory item, in Context B it is a purchasable catalog item. These are NOT the same entity."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A domain concept currently MISSING — a time-limited claim on inventory that bridges the gap between order placement and warehouse picking. Neither context currently owns this."},{"term":"Available-to-Promise (ATP)","definition":"The quantity of stock that can be committed to new orders, calculated as StockLevel minus already-reserved quantities. This concept does not yet exist and is critical for correctness."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"A read-model projection of warehouse stock levels into the sales context — inherently eventually consistent, not real-time."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel in Context A must never go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — you cannot ship items that do not exist. If violated, the warehouse data is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"For any Product, after any operation, StockLevel >= 0."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order in Context B must not be synchronously coupled to Context A's StockLevel at placement time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Bounded context autonomy is an architectural invariant. If Context B directly queries or writes to Context A, both contexts lose independent deployability and availability — a failure in the warehouse system would prevent all order placement, which is a worse business outcome than accepting an order that may need to be back-ordered.","testable_assertion":"Context B can accept an order submission even when Context A is completely unavailable."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to the sales team must be acknowledged as eventually consistent, not real-time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a display policy. The staleness tolerance (e.g., 30 seconds, 5 minutes) is a business decision a VP can tune. True real-time would require synchronous coupling that violates BR-2.","testable_assertion":"The stock indicator displays a 'last updated' timestamp, and the UI communicates that the value is approximate."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Blocking an order due to insufficient stock requires a Stock Reservation concept — checking stock and placing the order must be an atomic domain operation or use a saga/process manager.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without a reservation, two concurrent orders can both see stock=1, both pass the check, and both get accepted — violating the intent of the feature. This is a consistency invariant.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X with StockLevel=1, when two orders for Quantity=1 are placed concurrently, exactly one is accepted and one is blocked or queued."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A Stock Reservation must have a time-to-live (TTL) after which it expires and releases the reserved quantity back to available stock.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business policy — the TTL duration is configurable. Without it, abandoned carts or failed payments permanently reduce ATP, but the specific duration is a business decision.","testable_assertion":"A reservation created at time T that is not confirmed by T+TTL is automatically released, and ATP increases accordingly."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The integration must use a domain event or anti-corruption layer — Context B must NOT directly reference Context A's StockLevel entity.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Direct coupling between bounded contexts corrupts the domain model. Context B must consume a translated representation (e.g., a StockAvailabilityChanged event projected into its own read model). If violated, the system has domain drift and shared-kernel coupling that was not agreed upon.","testable_assertion":"Context B's codebase contains zero imports or direct references to Context A's domain entities."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places an order for Product X with Quantity 5","when":"Context A's Available-to-Promise for Product X is >= 5","then":"The order is accepted and a Stock Reservation for 5 units of Product X is created with a TTL"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer places an order for Product X with Quantity 5","when":"Context A's Available-to-Promise for Product X is < 5","then":"The order is blocked (not rejected outright — queued or flagged) and the customer is informed that stock is insufficient"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Context A (Warehouse Management) is temporarily unavailable","when":"A customer places an order","then":"The order is accepted provisionally and flagged for stock verification when Context A recovers — orders are NOT rejected due to infrastructure failure"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Two customers simultaneously place orders that together exceed available stock","when":"Both orders are processed","then":"At most one order is confirmed; the other is blocked or queued. StockLevel never goes below zero."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Stock Reservation was created but the order is abandoned or payment fails","when":"The reservation TTL expires","then":"The reserved quantity is released back to Available-to-Promise"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The sales team views the order management screen","when":"Stock levels change in Context A","then":"The stock indicator updates within the agreed staleness tolerance and displays a 'last updated' timestamp"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"Context B's codebase","when":"Inspected for cross-context references","then":"There are zero direct imports, API calls, or database queries to Context A's internal domain model — all integration goes through events or an anti-corruption layer"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Supplier receipt arrives (increasing stock) at the exact moment an order is being evaluated — stock check races with stock update.","expected_behavior":"The system uses the ATP at the moment of reservation creation. The receipt increases ATP for subsequent orders, not retroactively for in-flight evaluations."},{"scenario":"A multi-line order where some lines have sufficient stock and others do not.","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: block the entire order (all-or-nothing) or allow partial fulfillment. This is an unresolved ambiguity — see escalations."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Context B's catalog but has never been stocked in Context A (no StockLevel record).","expected_behavior":"ATP is treated as zero. The order is blocked. The system must not throw an error — absence of stock data means no stock."},{"scenario":"Stock indicator shows 'available' but by the time the customer submits the order, stock has been reserved by another order.","expected_behavior":"The order is blocked at submission time based on actual ATP, not the stale indicator. The UI must communicate that availability shown is approximate."},{"scenario":"Reservation TTL expires mid-payment-processing.","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: extend TTL during active payment, or hard-expire and force re-reservation. See escalations."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling (reducing refund/compensation costs) but risks lost revenue if ATP calculations are too conservative or the system is too aggressive in blocking. The reservation TTL directly impacts conversion rates — too short loses sales, too long locks up inventory.","operational":"Introduces significant operational complexity: a new Reservation concept, a saga or process manager, an event pipeline between contexts, and a read-model projection. Warehouse operations now have a downstream dependency — stock adjustments must publish events reliably. Failure modes multiply.","user":"Sales team gets visibility into stock (positive), but the 'real-time' expectation must be managed — eventual consistency will cause occasional stale data. Customers may see 'in stock' and then get blocked at checkout, creating frustration if the staleness window is too large.","risk":"HIGH. This integration couples two previously independent bounded contexts. The primary risks are: (1) synchronous coupling causing cascade failures, (2) race conditions on concurrent orders, (3) domain model corruption if anti-corruption layers are skipped for expediency, (4) reservation leaks if TTL management fails.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"May experience order blocking where previously all orders were accepted. Could see stale stock indicators leading to frustration. Requires clear UX communication."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock visibility (positive) but must understand the indicator is approximate. Workflow changes — blocked orders need a new handling process."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Stock adjustments now trigger downstream events. New operational responsibility to ensure events are published. Reservation expiry creates new stock movements to monitor."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Must build and maintain cross-context integration infrastructure: event bus, read-model projections, saga/process manager, reservation lifecycle management."},{"group":"Finance","impact":"Fewer oversell refunds (positive), but potential lost revenue from incorrectly blocked orders needs monitoring."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The feature request contains two significant ambiguities (partial fulfillment policy, reservation TTL behavior during payment) and one fundamental contradiction (requesting 'real-time' stock visibility across bounded contexts, which is architecturally impossible without violating context autonomy). The spec also fails to define who owns the new Reservation concept — it sits at the boundary of both contexts. These gaps make it impossible to write complete, correct acceptance criteria.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 invariant is non-negotiable","Anti-corruption layer is required between contexts","Eventual consistency is the only viable approach for the stock indicator","A Reservation concept is needed to prevent race conditions"],"low_confidence_areas":["Partial vs. all-or-nothing order fulfillment policy","Reservation TTL duration and behavior during active payment","Exactly which context owns the Reservation aggregate","Whether 'block' means reject, queue, or flag for manual review"],"assumptions":["The business is willing to accept eventual consistency for the stock indicator rather than true real-time","Orders that cannot be fulfilled should be blocked rather than accepted and back-ordered","The current architecture supports asynchronous event communication between contexts"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The feature request says 'automatically block it if stock is insufficient' but does not define what 'block' means for a multi-line order where some products have sufficient stock and others do not. Is this all-or-nothing (entire order blocked) or partial fulfillment (available lines proceed, insufficient lines blocked)?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Team"],"options":["All-or-nothing: block entire order if any line has insufficient stock","Partial fulfillment: accept lines with stock, block/backorder lines without","Customer choice: present options to customer at checkout"],"recommendation":"All-or-nothing is simpler and safer for initial implementation. Partial fulfillment is better UX but dramatically increases domain complexity (split orders, partial shipments, partial payments). Recommend all-or-nothing for v1."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real-time stock available indicator' is requested, but true real-time requires synchronous coupling between bounded contexts, which violates context autonomy (BR-2). The spec needs to define an acceptable staleness tolerance.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["Synchronous query to Context A (violates bounded context autonomy, creates availability coupling)","Event-driven read model with sub-second staleness (complex but preserves autonomy)","Polling-based read model with configurable refresh interval (simpler, 5-30 second staleness)"],"recommendation":"Event-driven read model with explicit 'last updated' timestamp in the UI. Staleness tolerance should be defined by the sales team — likely 5-30 seconds is acceptable."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No guidance on who owns the Stock Reservation aggregate. It sits at the boundary between Warehouse Management and Sales. Without clear ownership, the reservation logic will be duplicated or placed incorrectly, leading to domain corruption.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Warehouse Team","Sales Team"],"options":["Context A owns reservations (warehouse allocates stock)","Context B owns reservations (sales claims stock)","New bounded context: Inventory Allocation, owns the reservation lifecycle"],"recommendation":"Introduce a lightweight Inventory Allocation context or process manager that coordinates between A and B. Reservations are inherently cross-context — forcing them into either existing context will corrupt that context's model."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"What happens when a reservation's TTL expires while the customer is actively completing payment? Expiring the reservation loses the sale; extending it indefinitely defeats the purpose of TTL.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Finance"],"options":["Hard expiry: reservation expires regardless, customer must re-attempt order","Soft extension: if payment is in progress, extend TTL by a grace period (e.g., 5 minutes)","Payment-aware TTL: integrate with payment gateway status to hold reservation while payment is processing"],"recommendation":"Soft extension with a maximum cap (e.g., original TTL + 10 minutes hard limit). This balances customer experience with inventory availability."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The feature request implies synchronous coupling between Context A and Context B by requiring real-time stock checks at order placement time.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2 — Bounded context autonomy. If implemented naively, a warehouse system outage would prevent all order placement, which is a worse business outcome than occasional overselling."},{"violation":"The feature request does not account for the Stock Reservation concept, without which concurrent orders will race and the stock-blocking invariant cannot be reliably enforced.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — Atomic reservation required. Without it, the feature gives a false sense of safety while allowing the exact overselling it claims to prevent."}]}

This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling problem that fundamentally changes the autonomy of both contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
